That the person who is as it were, leading should be a real leader, a genuine leader, not just someone artificially put in a position of authority, and exercising power. This is what power means.

I've never discussed this but I have thought about it quite a bit. Power, in this rather negative sense, means something quite artificial exercised by someone by mere virtue of the position, not by virtue of their intrinsic individuality - do you see what I mean?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I think you have talked about this before.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Have I?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: In relation to Tibetan hierarchy.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Mm' may be.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: And some people coming out of Tibet could ...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's true, that's very true, yes.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That's because they weren't supported by the whole power thing, and couldn't operate with other people in a real, genuinely...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, right. So personal influence as distinct from power due to position, yes. So that the person in the position as it were, of power, should be the person with individual influence. Influence in the sense of force of personality, individuality. I mean for instance when you get some monstrous kings. I was reading a bit about, the early 'Georges'. I was reading the life of Handel recently, and George the First, and George the Second. I mean, everybody despised them. They were the Kings, Kings of England. There they were strutting around in their garter
and their ribbons and all that sort of thing. Everybody bowing to them. But no one respected them. People despised them in fact. But, they were the king, George the First and George the Second.

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: That often leads to a lot of corruption because you have to keep bypassing that power.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right. And that person, though so unsuitable, has power, can do things. So when you get a person in that sort of position who is not suited to that position and is exercising power by virtue of his position and not by virtue of his individuality this is a source of corruption, of downfall in every way. Like when a young playboy is installed, as guru maharaj - the perfect master. A good example! Downfall, for himself and his followers. Or when you've got the Borgia popes. Well you get it in government all the time.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: In terms of ecclesiastical hierarchy you could have, not necessarily bad people, but just ordinary people, in high positions.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Just ordinary people, yes. Right, oh, they don't have to be bad, if they are mediocre they do enough harm. (Pause.) That applies even on a very small scale, even if you have a little committee, and that the person who is supposed to be in charge of the committee isn't really able to run it, well, it doesn't function.
''Tis truly so we know, the eleventh suffering. Now tell us sir the twelfth, the source of suffering.
'When born of noble clan, a man is poor and craves For much and longs to rule, a source of suffering that.'

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What do you think the Buddha is talking about here?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It's not really ambition is it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It is ambition, it is political ambition, political aggression and aggrandizement.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: If he is born of noble clan how can he be poor?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well he may be the younger son.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Mm! Yes, or the black sheep or something!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. I mean, the fact that he is born of a noble clan gives him a certain ambitiousness. He takes it for granted he should have a prominent position in society, but he is poor. So, he embarks upon a career of expansion and conquest. So one can apply this at various levels.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Ambition isn't a bad thing, is it really?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, there, there is no word for ambition here, we've just used that word. We are using it in the negative sense. But there can be a positive ambition, one can give the word ambition a
positive meaning, but it won't work itself out in conquering other people and other lands.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: This is a craving, isn't it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Because perhaps of the inner emptiness, the personal poverty as it were.
So then the Buddha concludes:
'These sufferings in the world the wise discern, And blessed with vision Aryan, They seek the world of bliss.'

In other words, having seen these sources of downfall, they direct their efforts to what is positive, and happy. The word for bliss is 'sivan', which is the same as the god 'Shiva'. (Transcriber's note: there is no 'h' in the Pali text.) It means not only what is blissful, but also what is auspicious, good, positive. What sort of general impression do you get from this series of verses?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It does feel quite like the Mangala Sutta.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In reverse!

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Yes!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The Mangala Sutta in reverse, yes.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: That sort of level.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Very practical, down to earth.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Who would it have been recited to? Is it a sort of, the laity or lay people?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It seems to apply more to the laity doesn't it than to the monks as it were.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: They are very sort of, not exactly obvious but straight forward do's and don'ts.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, the Mangala Sutta in a way, is directed more to the laity isn't it?

turns.speaker: Siddhiratna
paragraphs: I've forgotten which the Mangala Sutta was ...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The Mangala Sutta is the one where 'tell us the most auspicious sign of all' or 'tell us the greatest blessing.' And the Buddha says 'Not to associate with fools, but to associate with the wise. The worship of those deserving worship, that is the greatest blessing.
: We haven't done it yet Siddhi.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We haven't done it here, no. It's in another work. One certainly gets the impression of down-to-earthness. A direct address to people living in the world: pointing out very clearly
and simply what are the sort of things that contribute to people's deterioration, and downfall, ruin, disaster, from an ethical and spiritual point of view.
: Is there any particular significance do you think in the fact there is a conversation with a devi?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I wondered about that.
: The Mangala Sutta is also, isn't it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: 'Deva' in the Mangala Sutta. Many suttas are according to the tradition addressed to devas or devis, I mean the Buddhist would say 'well it just happened to be a devi who asked the Buddha these questions!' [255] It's as simple as that. May be there is no mystery or particular meaning in it at all. (Pause.) Some of you seem a bit tired and have probably had a long day. I think it might be a good idea if we have a quiet cup of tea, and then finish a little early, what do you say to that? And you can all go to bed. Someone put the kettle on.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well there is no reason why we shouldn't continue next time I come down.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Let's make a date now! (Laughter.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No! (Laughter.) If you say things like that I'll go off to Formenterra and do them with Devaraja (Laughter.)

turns.speaker: Dhammadinna
paragraphs: We will have to get you your own private jet-plane.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No. I think some of these verses he might not like so much! But you can see the strange way in which some of the words are translated. It says 'Of Suffering', suffering from 'Parabhava'. It's quite misleading. Anyone who just had a cursory acquaintance of Buddhist texts and translations, would think it was about 'dukkha', but it isn't. It's about 'downfall', the opposite of progress, the opposite of individual development.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: It sounds as if it has been translated by a bit of a puritan.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In a way.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Translating it as 'suffering'.

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: It's suffering seen from the outside, isn't it, by instances of downfall.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: How are you going on with your translation of the Dhammapada?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I am not going on at all I am afraid, but when I do get some time I am going to, well if there is something I could do, if I went to Formenterra. If I spent a whole month, just on that, I could finish it quite easily, quite comfortably.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: What about all the other things then?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, that's it. But it will get done in due course, all being well. If I don't fall under the proverbial bus or something like that, it will all get done.

turns.speaker: Marichi
paragraphs: Are you going to Formenterra or is this just a sort of carrot to get you to do the Dhammapada?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well Devaraja has fallen in love with Formenterra and he wants to take all his friends there. So he has been urging me to go and he has been trying to persuade Chintamani to go and I told him that I might be tempted if I had some work which I could take with me for a month and do it there. Possibly I could do the Dhammapada there. I'd quite like the warm climate. But I couldn't go before the Winter time. They want to have some study retreats in the Autumn.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: What are you going to study, have you any idea?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't really know. There are many possibilities. We will just see. I think we talk a lot about the Higher Evolution, and quite rightly so, but I think we don't perhaps very often consider the opposite possibility - that is to say the 'downfall'. Perhaps we should be a bit more mindful of that possibility - that you can fall back. If you haven't passed the point of no-return, you can fall right back and right down. But if you are in regular contact with a spiritual community it's highly unlikely because your friends will just hang on to you; they won't let you fall any further. But if you are not in contact with a spiritual community, if you are left to your own resources you could, you can fall right back and right down.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: If you have become alienated from the spiritual community, it's quite difficult for members of that community to get through or...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Indeed, yes.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I mean, as we've seen in recent past with several resignations.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, quite.
: Contact implies that you are not an alienated person, to be in contact.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I don't know, contact - it seems to...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But usually, even the most alienated person has some order member or other in whom he or she confides a bit more than in others, and it's up to that particular person to do what he or she can.
: So it's important to have friends.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Important to have friends, yes. But especially as the order gets bigger, much as one may wish to do so, one can't be equally friends in the full practical concrete sense with everybody. You will have your own little circle, without being cliquish or anything like that. That circle may even change from time to time as people come and go. But you can't remain on, as I said, practical concrete terms of close friendship with more than a certain number of people. I

mean in your meditation, in your metta you wish well to all. You can even quite genuinely feel very, very much at one with all, but you can't usually translate that into terms of practice if there is more than a certain number of people involved. There are limitations of time if nothing else.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: You relate to some just on a (easier, automatically)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. Again one shouldn't necessarily follow the line of least resistance and just associate with people you happen to like and tend to avoid others, even though they may be nearer at hand just because they are not your sort or something like that sort. The basic fact that they also have gone for refuge means there is something very, very deeply in common. You should be able to relate via that common factor. However unpromising they may seem, or however different their type may be from yours, or their interest may be from yours. Sometimes it does happen that you relate very well to very different people, but sometimes not. But in any case you can't concretely relate and regularly relate to more than a certain number. May be ten or twelve is [257] the limit, even four or five sometimes. Say four or five really good friends, well you've got a lot. (Pause)
But if circumstances just happen to put you into contact with certain order members, it should be possible to include them in your closer circle. (pause.)

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: It is quite important to have that very close friendship within the Order, because otherwise even though in the Order one has a lot of contact one can at times be undernourished.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You can know lots of people but have no friends.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: You can even be getting on quite well with people but still not get a necessary nourishment.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, that's true.

turns.speaker: Siddhiratna
paragraphs: What do you think the nature of necessary nourishment is? I am not quite sure, I know that I do get it from certain people.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: That sort of sparking off thing.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's a more spontaneous type of relationship about nothing in particular that you can relate with them and to them, even though there is nothing to discuss, there is no council business to discuss, or no meditation problem to discuss. But you just find yourself with them and you are so happy and have a spontaneous relationship.

turns.speaker: Lokamitra
paragraphs: I find with me is, it tends to be people I've known for some time, may be have been through things with, or not, so emotionally they are reliable in that I (?)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think other factors being equal your best friends are your old friends. I mean you do definitely build up over the years I am sure of this.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There is a knowledge of each other as well, an ease with each other.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Someone was telling me, I forget who it was, but, a little while ago, not so long ago, I think it was Ananda, quite sort of by chance, as it were, a number of people who had been at Aryatara in the old days just happened to be reunited there. And they have a very strong feeling for one another, even though they had not been living together for quite a few years now. (There) was Ananda and Malini and who else?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Gotami?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I am not sure whether Gotami was there. It was four or five of them. I think Dharmapala happened to be there.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: (Inaudible)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And they felt so close, even though they had not been living under the same roof for several years. And they felt very much at ease with one another, and very much in harmony. (Pause.) I think this is also important why the working team should not be disturbed too often. You need to be working with people for a sufficient length of time to get to know them really well in that particular way; say for instance two years is a good time. If you work with somebody regularly over a period of two years well, you can say you know that person quite well then. And even if you separate after that, and go your own ways, when you do meet up again, you can as it were, carry on from where you left off. (Pause.)
(Transcriber's note: I have not transcribed a few lines which have to do with domestic matters and cats!)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: What is happening over the weekend here?

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: Just our usual class on Sunday evening.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: I thought I'd organize a metta bhavana at the time of the private ordination.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's very good. Yes. That will be, let me think, let me give you the time. It will be starting seven-o'clock to-morrow evening, and then the public ordination at twelve o'clock.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: (Inaudible.)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There will be three being ordained, so the whole thing should go on from seven until nine I guess. The metta bhavana starting at seven (inaudible) (and at) eight o'clock or just over eight o'clock, and then after that there will be winding up with the meditation and puja. I take it everybody knows that it's Richard (Hooson) and Graham Carter and Colin Ferguson. And Colin has moved into Sukhavati and he is away on retreat at the moment. When he comes back he will be a permanent as it were member of Sukhavati, carrying on the work on the image with Chintamani.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: There will be quite a large Order community there then won't there be?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, quite large. Especially when Ratnapani and Vajrakumara come down, which they will I think, on Monday.
(Transcriber's note; chit-chat about Sukhavati not transcribed here as it is just domestic chit-chat!)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I saw by the way, this afternoon the clay model of the image that Greta has done for the West London Centre. It's very, very beautiful and I was very pleased with it, very suitable I think. I don't know what others make of it, but I certainly like it very much.

It's a Shakyamuni in meditation posture. It's rather solid and simple with nice clear clean lines. But the face is very beautiful, very meditative feeling to it. It's done with great sensitivity. At the same time it's quite solid, if you know ...
: It's not wishy-washy?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Not in the least wishy-washy, no.
: It's not heavy either.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's not heavy, and it's done in an interesting sort of way technically. It will be cast, but I think it will be very successful. It's a good size too. A very successful image I think. And Greta herself always keeps one copy, for her own collection as it were. She has got some really beautiful work.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: She is a good sculptress.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think she really is good, yes. She is really, you know, a professional.

turns.speaker: Unknown
paragraphs: What was your impress ion of her generally?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Very positive, very good warm feelings. Very free and open feelings.

I would say, probably it's the best image so far made within the Movement as it were. I'd go so far as to say that. It's very good work. It is also quite a youthful Buddha especially if you look at the figure from the side. It a very young Buddha, and the features are not oriental. At the same time it's not, as it were, too obviously 'Western'. You don't think of that: it does not strike you as Eastern, at the same time you don't think 'ah there is a Western-type Buddha.'
(End of tape is chit-chat; not transcribed here about the Image.')
END OF SEMINAR
[Spellchecked and put into house style, Shantavira August 2000]
